title: RA.1005 Lucrecia Dalt
tracklist:

Charlie Haden, Keith Jarrett - Ellen David

Tindersticks - The Organist Entertains

Lucrecia Dalt - Covenstead Blues

translation.content: Lucrecia Dalt isn't your typical electronic artist. The Colombian singer and composer approaches music-making in the way a fantasy writer builds worlds. Over the past two decades, she's produced a catalogue that reads more like a bookshelf of strange, interlinked novels, each with its own laws, characters and textures, extending the one before it. Born in the Andean city of Pereira, Dalt has lived in Barcelona, Berlin and now New Mexico. This globe-trotting informs a remarkable restlessness across her work, from the Colombian beast that haunted her sixth album, Anticlines, to the disembodied voice of her protagonist, Lia, that carried the 2020 LP, No era sólida. Her Latin heritage has always been close at hand, resurfacing most vividly on 2022's ¡Ay!, where she recast bolero, son and salsa through the lens of science fiction. Her latest full-length, A Danger to Ourselves, marks another shift, this time away from fantastical characters and back towards her own image, centering desire, vulnerability and the intensity of new love. Dalt's RA Mix is a fascinating entry into the series (and will sit comfortably with RA's recent archival playlist, Mixes From Artists Who Don't Call Themselves DJs, But Probably Should). Take the opening track, "Cellophane," by Beak>, the band led by Portishead's Geoff Barrow. The lyrics set the tone for the hour to come: "Now the wind has blown down / Now the truth is laid out there." True to Dalt's oeuvre, RA.1005 has little regard for convention. Kick drums and beatmatching are nowhere to be heard; instead, she offers a collage of inspiration, drawing connections across eras, moods and geographies. The mix includes the work of close collaborators (David Sylvian, Juana Molina and Niño de Elche) as well as excursions into psychedelic jazz (Lloyd's Miller's "Gol-E-Gandom"), sombre downtempo (Muslimgauze's "Enchante, Monsieur") and Korean pop (Leenalchi's "Magic Pocket). Spanning just over an hour, it unfolds like another chapter in Dalt's ongoing project of world-building through sound. - Bella Aquilina What have you been up to recently? Sunset walks in the desert. How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? I started compiling ideas at LAX Airport. I love working on projects like this while on a plane because I feel so focused, and the enclosed space serves as the perfect canvas to free my mind. The mix was then finalised in my home studio. I enjoy making mixes because they become statements of a moment—something I'm obsessed with and continue to be obsessed with—and because I approach them as I would mixing a song, using FX and working hard to make samples or recordings, in this case "songs," cohere rhythmically and melodically. What are you looking forward to in the near future? Performing again! And to gather ideas for a new album. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? GASC Foundation What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? I'm no longer a club-oriented person, but I do love dancing. What I genuinely enjoy currently is spontaneous kitchen parties. The other day, I made a parmigiana for two friends. While waiting for a piece of eggplant to cook properly, we danced around the kitchen table to some of the songs featured on this mix. I also have a very fond memory of seeing Mika Vainio at Berghain—an experience that made me rethink the affect of sound. Your new album, A Danger to Ourselves, feels more personal than your past records. What was it like to let go of characters and concepts and write from your own experience? The act of being vulnerable and raw, an acknowledgment of the fragility and intimacy of our human experience. It's about embracing the personal as universal, exposing oneself to the possibility of both loss and continuity. You've drawn on bolero, son, salsa, and other Latin styles in your work. How do you think your relationship to that music has changed over the years? It evolves like memory itself—the more I age, the more I value growing up in Colombia and the rich culture we have. Since you're not a DJ in the traditional sense, how does making a mix compare to making an album? A mix at LAX unfolded like a snapshot of a moment in transit—ideas of possibility that suggest a mood. Then, I treat songs like samples on an album, building the narrative to make it cohesive and emotionally entangled.
translation.blurb: A rare mix from the critically acclaimed experimentalist.
